content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 21,
1994
David B.
Cosgrove
Rutan
& Tucker
P.O.
Box 1950
Costa Mesa,
CA
92626-1998
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our Advice File No. A-94-314
Dear Mr.
Cosgrove:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Signal Hill City Councilman Richard Ceccia under the conflict—of—
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) •1/
This letter is based on the facts presented to us in your
September 21,
1994 letter as well as information provided in our
telephone conversation of October 18,
1994.
QUESTION
Does Mr.
Ceccia’s employment as the in—house counsel with the
California State University System in Long Beach
(“CSULB”)
prevent
him from participating in decisions as a councilmember to develop
an educational/research/training facility which may be operated by
CSULB?
CONCLUSION
Since the definition of “income” excludes salary from any
state, local or federal govermnent agency,
Mr.
Ceccia’s status as
an employee with CSUIB does not create a disqualifying financial
interest in a governmental decision.
FACTS
The City of Signal Hill and the City of Long Beach are
contemplating forming a joint powers agency to pursue development
of property which straddles the boundary of both Signal Hill and
Long Beach.
The purpose of the joint powers agency would be to
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice No.
A-94—314
Page
2
negotiate with the developer on the development of a hotel,
automobile race track testing facility,
and industrial research
center.
One of the proposals may include development of an
educational/research/training facility to be operated by an
educational institution.
The educational institution has not yet
been selected, but may include the California State University at
Long Beach,
or a nonprofit corporation affiliated with CSULB.
Richard Ceccia is a Signal Hill City Councilman and is also
employed by CSULB as its in—house counsel.
Because of the
potential involvement of CSUIB in the development proposal,
Mr.
Ceccia is requesting advice regarding potential conflicts of
interest.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.
An official has a financial
interest in a governmental decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the
official’s immediate family,
or on:
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(Sections 87103(a)
and
(d))
The definition of income means a payment received,
including
but not limited to any salary,
wage,
advance, dividend,
interest,
rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, etc.
(Section 82020(a).)
However,
the Act expressly exempts salary from a state, local or
federal government agency from the definition of “income.”
(Section 82030(b) (2).)
Mr.
Ceccia is employed as the counsel to
CSULB,
a state agency.
Thus, his salary from CSULB does not
create a conflict of interest.
The definition of “business entity” as used in Section
87103(d)
above means any organization or enterprise operated for
profit,
including but not limited to a proprietorship,


Advice No.
A—94—314
Page
3
partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate,
corporation or assoàiation.
(Section 82005.)
CSULB is not a
profit-making entity,
and therefore is not a business entity.
Mr.
Ceccia employment with CSUIJB does not create a conflict of
interest.
Thus,
absent some other disqualifying financial interest as
set forth in Section 87103,
above,
or some direct financial effect
on Mr.
Ceccia personally resulting from the governmental
decisions,
Mr.
Ceccia may participate in decisions concerning the
proposed educational/research/training facility.
I trust this answers your question.
If you have any further
questions,
or need to discuss this, please feel free to contact
the Commission’s Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven C.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By,
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
SGC/JET/jt

an employee with CSUIB does not create a disqualifying financial
interest in a governmental decision.

QUESTION: Does Mr.
Ceccia’s employment as the in—house counsel with the
California State University System in Long Beach
(“CSULB”)
prevent
him from participating in decisions as a councilmember to develop
an educational/research/training facility which may be operated by
CSULB?

CONCLUSION: Since the definition of “income” excludes salary from any
state, local or federal govermnent agency,
Mr.
Ceccia’s status as
an employee with CSUIB does not create a disqualifying financial
interest in a governmental decision.
